behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: the failure is at the watcher/OS event boundary (recursive watch behavior), not just a local parser. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: recursive delivery of subdirectory events is OS- and watcher-implementation-dependent; mocked events can falsely pass. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: this could be Integration if the watcher library already guarantees recursive events and you only test internal routing, but the behavior text explicitly targets real watch coverage of subdirectories, so System remains the minimum. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because the text requires real subdirectory watch behavior, which in-process stubs do not validate.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp input directory, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this behavior is fundamentally dedup key/time-window logic for one component and can fail entirely within that object boundary. REAL-INFRA: no OS/network/subprocess is required. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: could be Integration if dedup state is spread across queue + dispatcher modules, but the behavior text is specifically about dedup logic correctness for same path/window, which is directly unit-testable.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate dedup component with a fixed clock, submit the same path twice within the configured window, and assert first is accepted and second is discarded; then advance clock past window and assert acceptance.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: duplicate-event bursts are a real watcher/OS behavior emerging at module boundaries. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: mocked watcher callbacks can miss true event burst patterns and timing, hiding duplicate-processing bugs. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: Integration might catch this if you inject duplicate events manually, but that does not prove behavior under real OS emission characteristics named explicitly in the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform one physical write operation known to generate multiple events on the target platform, and assert exactly one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is a lifecycle handoff between daemon and real filesystem watcher state after delete/recreate. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: directory removal/recreation semantics and watch invalidation are OS-level; mocks can hide missed re-subscription bugs. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: Integration could validate internal rebind logic with fake events, but the behavior requires proving recovery from actual directory disappearance/recreation while live.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon on a real directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: ordering depends on handoff across watcher intake, metadata lookup, and queue scheduling (multi-module boundary), so Unit is too low. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no required real network/subprocess/OS primitive is stated beyond local module wiring; no AI dependency; no multi-agent sequence. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: System could be needed if correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp granularity/clock quirks, but the behavior text constrains expectation to ordering by timestamps, which can be validated with controlled in-process metadata fixtures.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake + metadata + scheduler modules with an in-process filesystem adapter/fake stats, enqueue simultaneous arrivals with different creation timestamps, and assert processing order follows timestamp ascending/defined policy.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is a race between real write progress and watcher-triggered pickup across OS/filesystem behavior. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: partial writes, file locks/flush timing, and event timing are hard to reproduce faithfully with mocks; stubs often hide premature-read bugs. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: Integration could test retry/backoff policy with a fake 'file busy' signal, but that does not validate the true timing/interleaving failure described.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real directory, write a large file in chunks (or hold stream open), trigger arrival early, and assert transformation starts only after write stream closes and full file content is available.
